qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: CBOR RegExpDecoder creates arbitrary RegExp from untrusted CBOR data enabling ReDoS
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory describes stack exhaustion DoS from deeply nested CBOR arrays, maps, or tags without depth limits. This finding describes a different vulnerability: ReDoS via automatic decoding of CBOR tag 35 (RegExp) with unvalidated patterns. While both vulnerabilities affect @stablelib/cbor, they have different root causes (recursive descent without depth limit vs. unsafe RegExp construction from tag decoder) and different attack vectors (deep nesting vs. malicious regex pattern).

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: X25519 sharedKey defaults to rejectZero=false enabling small subgroup attacks with predictable session keys
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory describes a stack exhaustion vulnerability in @stablelib/cbor. This finding describes a cryptographic weakness in @stablelib/x25519, which is a different package entirely. The root cause, vulnerable code, attack vector, and impact are all unrelated to the CBOR decoder stack exhaustion issue.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: None of the qualifying findings identify the advisory-described vulnerability: stack exhaustion DoS from deeply nested CBOR arrays, maps, or tags due to unlimited recursion in @stablelib/cbor. The cbor-redos-regexp finding is in the same package but describes a different vulnerability (ReDoS via RegExp tag decoder). The x25519-small-subgroup finding is in a different package entirely.
evidence: Advisory describes stack exhaustion from deeply nested CBOR structures without depth limit
Advisory PoC shows payload with 12000 nested arrays causing RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded
cbor-redos-regexp finding describes ReDoS via tag 35 (RegExp) decoder, not stack exhaustion from nesting depth
x25519-small-subgroup finding describes a cryptographic weakness in a different package (@stablelib/x25519)
